title: A Vibrio cholerae Type IV restriction system targets glucosylated 5-hydroxymethylcytosine to protect against phage infection

INTRODUCTION.content: Cholera, an acute diarrheal disease that is caused byVibrio cholerae, has greatly impacted human history. Today, it is estimated that 1.3 to 4 millionV.choleraeinfections occur annually worldwide, leading to around 21,000 to 143,000 deaths (1,2). A major evolutionary selectionV. choleraemust overcome is predation by bacteriophages (phage) in both its aquatic environment and pathogenic lifestyle (3).V. choleraeepidemics are intricately associated with phage infections as phage blooms curtail cholera outbreaks (4). Three dominant lytic phages, ICP1, ICP2, and ICP3, have been identified to eradicateV. choleraebothin vivoand during environmental persistence (3). However, given the ubiquitous nature ofV. choleraein environmental reservoirs, it is curious that more diverse phages targeting this bacterium have not been discovered, suggesting thatV. choleraeis adept at defending against phage infection.

To overcome phage predation,V. choleraehas acquired and/or evolved a myriad of phage defense systems (5–8). Interestingly, these phage defense systems are intricately linked to genomic regions or prophages acquired by horizontal gene transfer, including theVibriopathogenicity islands (VPI-1 and VPI-2) (9,10) andVibrioseventh pandemic island (VSP-1 and VSP-2) (11,12). The VSP-1 and VSP-2 islands are only found in the current 7th pandemic El Tor biotype strains, and their association with phage defense supports that these islands allow for long-term persistence, and potentially the emergence of this pandemic (13–15). VSP-1 encodes a recently identified phage defense system,avcID (6). AvcI is a noncoding RNA antitoxin that inhibits the cytidine deaminase activity of AvcD (6). Upon the inhibition of host gene transcription, AvcI is rapidly degraded, liberating AvcD to deplete free deoxycytidine nucleotides within the cell, decreasing phage replication (16). VSP-I also encodesdncV, capV, vc0180,andvc0181,acyclic-oligonucleotide-basedanti-phagesignalingsystem (CBASS) phage defense system, which limits phage infection to the surrounding population through abortive infection (8,17,18). VSP-2 encodes the genesddmABC,which are DNA condensins that have been shown to limit both phage replication and plasmid replication (7,12). A partner system,ddmDE,that also limits plasmid acquisition and potentially phage infection is encoded on VPI-2 (7).V. choleraealso encodes a phage-inducible chromosomal-like element (PLE) that inhibits replication of ICP1 by confiscating its replication machinery (19–21)

Given these diverse defense systems, it is interesting that restriction/modification systems (R-M), which have been appreciated for decades as mediating phage defense (22), have only been recently identified inV. cholerae (23). These enzymes are grouped into four classes. Canonical restriction enzymes that belong to Classes I–III, which are widely used in molecular biology and digest unmodified DNA at a specific sequence (24,25). The DNA of the host is protected by a cognate methylase that blocks the activity of the restriction enzyme, whereas unprotected phage DNA is rapidly degraded upon infection (24,25). In response to these defense systems, phage have evolved mechanisms to modify the bases of their genomes (26–30). For example, T-even coliphage (T2, T4, and T6) all specifically methylate their cytosines at the 5’ position to generate 5-hydroxymethylcytosine (5hmC), which blocks the activity of many R-M systems (31).

Such modifications are selected by Type IV class of restriction enzymes (25,28,32–34). Rather than targeting unmodified DNA, these enzymes specifically digest modified DNA associated with phage. For example, the McrA and McrBC restriction systems ofE. colidigest genomes with either 5-methylcytosine (5mC) or the 5hmC modification (32,35,36). In response to restriction enzymes like McrA and McrBC, phage evolved additional base modifications to prevent digestion, such as glucosylation, which adds either α- or β-glucose to 5hmC. However, other Type IV restriction enzymes, as demonstrated by GmrSD, evolved to specifically digest glucosylated 5hmC (37–40). Therefore, bacteria and phage have a complex evolutionary interplay between phage defense mediated by restriction enzymes and phage counter defense strategies that further alter the phage genome.

Given the relative lack of diversity of phages that have been identified to infectV. cholerae,we hypothesized that this bacterium encodes previously undescribed phage defense systems. To identify such systems, we implemented an unbiased forward genetic screen of aV. choleraegenomic cosmid library for genes that protectedEscherichia colifrom phage infection. Using this approach, we identified the genesvc1767andvc1766, renamed herein astgvABfor theType I-embeddedgmrSD-like system ofVPI-2, which provided broad protection against T-even phage. We find that TgvAB targets phage DNA with glucosylated 5-hydroxymethylcytosine (5hmC) bases. Phages can evolve to overcome TgvAB by null mutations in theiragtand/orbgtgenes encoding the α- or β-glucosyltransferase that glucosylates 5hmCs, respectively. However, phages that evolve resistance to TgvAB exhibit a significant tradeoff that leaves them vulnerable to restriction by the Type IV restriction system, McrA and McrBC, which targets unglucosylated 5hmC. We also found that the associated Type I R-M system can protect against several other phages. Our study adds an additional restriction enzyme to the phage defense repertoire ofV. choleraeand demonstrates the inherent evolutionary interplay between phage genome modification and restriction defense.

RESULTS.content: To identify novel phage defense systems in V. cholerae, we screened a V. cholerae genomic library encoding random ~25-kB fragments from V. cholerae’s genome inserted into the cosmid pLAFR for protection against phage infection in E. coli strain DH10β. We screened for phage defense activity in E. coli as we hypothesized the dominant vibriophages, ICP1, ICP2, and ICP3, may be inherently resistant to such systems or have evolved counter defense mechanisms, which contributes to their dominance. Additionally, we have shown that V. cholerae phage defense systems such as CBASS and AvcID can be expressed in E. coli and confer protection against well-studied coliphage ( 6 , 17 ). We therefore screened ~350 individual clones encoding the V. cholerae genomic library for resistance to T2 phage infection using a spot dilution assay measuring plaque formation. We chose T2 as our initial phage to screen as it demonstrates sensitivity to V. cholerae CBASS in E. coli ( 17 ). One cosmid, which we named pJBG007, conferred complete protection against T2 infection as compared to the sensitive pLAFR cosmid control ( Fig. 1A ). Additionally, infection of T2 at a multiplicity of infection (MOI) of 0.01 in liquid cultures of E. coli indicated that pJBG007 provided significant protection compared with pLAFR ( Fig. 1B ). At later time points, culture growth stopped and began to decline, suggesting the rise of phage resistance mutants in liquid culture. Fig 1 Fig 1 V. cholerae El Tor contains phage defense systems on VPI-2. A. Tenfold serial dilution plaque assay of T2 phage spotted on pJBG007, a cosmid containing vc1770-vc1762 compared to pLAFR (empty vector). B. E. coli grown with either empty vector (yellow line) or pJBG007 (blue line) infected at time point 0 with the T2 phage at an MOI of 0.01. The mean and standard error of four biological replicates each with three technical replicates are presented. C. Plaque-forming units (PFU/mL) for the indicated phage on E. coli containing the empty vector (light gray) or pJBG007 (dark gray). The dashed line denotes the limit of detection (LOD). A Welch’s t -test was used to compare the mean (seven biological replicates each with three technical replicates) PFU count for each phage between the empty vector and pJBG007. D. A schematic of VPI-2 region within pJBG007, highlighting transposon insertions A, B, C, and D for Fig. 2 , is shown. To test the breadth of pJBG007 protection against other phages, we quantified infection of nine additional coliphage against E. coli containing pJBG007. Quantification of plaque-forming units (PFU) indicated pJBG007 provided complete protection compared to pLAFR against T2, T4, T5, T6, and secΦ18, with no observable plaques at any dilution analyzed. Moreover, a moderate but statistically significant protection against T3, secΦ18, secΦ27, and λ was also observed ( Fig. 1C ). These results indicated that pJBG007 encodes genes that provide broad and significant protection against coliphage infection.

Sequencing of the genomic DNA present in pJBG007 revealed that a 16,765 basepair (bp) fragment encoded in the cosmid was part of VPI-2 ( Fig. 1D ). VPI-2 encodes the ddmDE biological defense system ( 7 ), but only ddmE was present on pJBG007. Previous results have shown that both ddmD and ddmE are required for the function of this system, indicating that ddmE likely was not contributing to the defense we observed ( 7 ). In addition to ddmE , this fragment encoded an annotated Type I R-M and a truncated newly described phage defense system known as Zorya ( Fig. 1D ), neither of which have been studied in V. cholerae . Based on the sequence homology, these genes would be considered a Type I Zorya system. Prior studies suggest Type I Zorya systems encode four genes, zorABCD , of which only zorABC are present in pJBG007 ( 41 ). Therefore, it is currently unclear if vc1764-vc1762 encodes a functional Zorya defense module. Phage defense systems cluster in defense islands ( 41 – 44 ), and therefore this fragment of DNA is a relatively unstudied defense island of V. cholerae . Recognizing that either the R-M system or Zorya system could be responsible for phage protection, we sought to identify the genes necessary for protection against T2.

To determine which genes were responsible for protection against T2 infection, we mutagenized pJBG007 with a transposon and tested ~50 transposon mutants for loss of defense against T2 infection. We identified four transposon mutants in pJBG007 that lost all defenses to T2 ( Fig. 2A ). These four transposon mutants also failed to protect E. coli infected in a liquid culture from T2 at an MOI of 0.01 ( Fig. 2B ). Sequencing of these transposon mutants identified two independent insertions in vc1767 and two independent insertions in vc1766 ( Fig. 1D ). vc1767 and vc1766 are in a putative operon between genes vc1769 and vc1765, which was annotated as a Type I R-M system ( Fig. 1D ) with homology to hsdMSR ( 24 , 45 ). Basic Local Alignment Search Tool (BLAST) analysis indicated that vc1767 and vc1766 are both part of the COG1479 protein family, which is a conserved family of Type IV restriction DNase/DNA nickases specific for phosphorothioated or glycosylated phage DNA. This family is represented by GmrSD, which targets glucosylated 5hmC bases and DndB/SspE, which targets phosphorothioated bases ( 29 , 46 ). vc1767 and vc1766 both encode a predicted DUF262 that sense modified DNA and HNH nuclease domains. Given this analysis and the results described below, we renamed vc1767 and vc1766 to T ype I-embedded g mrSD -like system of V PI-2, tgvA and tgvB , respectively. Fig 2 Fig 2 Disruption of tgvA, tgvB, and tgvAB causes loss of phage defense against T2. A. A tenfold serial dilution plaque assay of T2 phage spotted on the empty vector (pLAFR), pJBG007 (positive control), or the indicated mutants. B. Growth curves for the pLAFR, pJBG007, and the transposon mutants. C. tgvA or tgvB deletion mutants infected at time point 0 with T2 phage at an MOI of 0.01. The mean and standard error of four biological replicates each with three technical replicates are presented. To test if the transposon insertions were polar on the surrounding genes, we generated Δ tgvA, Δ tgvB, or Δ tgvAB deletions in pJBG007 and measured T2 infection. Because the open reading frame (ORF) of tgvA overlaps the ORF of tgvB by eight basepairs, we engineered the deletion of tgvA or tgvB, such that the other ORF remained intact. Each of these mutants had a complete loss of protection against T2 ( Fig. 2A ). Similarly, T2 infection at an MOI of 0.01 in liquid cultures showed that all three deletion mutants no longer protected against infection ( Fig. 2C ). Together, these results suggested that both tgvA and tgvB were necessary for protection against T2 phage.

To determine if tgvA , tgvB , or both genes were sufficient for phage defense, we created plasmids encoding tgvA, tgvB, and tgvAB expressed from their native promoter and screened for defense against T2 infection in E. coli . The p tgvB plasmid was made by amplifying a region 520 bp upstream of tgvA to downstream of the stop codon of tgvB using the Δ tgvA mutant as a DNA template, such that tgvB would be expressed from its native promoter without tgvA . For controls, we used pJBG007, which provides robust defense, and the corresponding empty vector pEVS. Plasmids expressing the individual genes, p tgvA and p tgvB, exhibit plaquing equivalent to the empty vector, indicating these genes alone do not protect against infection. However, p tgvAB expressing both genes showed protection to T2 equivalent to pJBG007 ( Fig. 3A ). Analogous results were obtained in a liquid infection assay with T2 at an MOI of 0.01, indicating only p tgvAB provided protection from infection ( Fig. 3B ). Fig 3 Fig 3 tgvAB confers protection against T2. A. A tenfold serial dilution plaque assay of T2 phage spotted on pEVS (empty vector), pJBG007, p tgvA , p tgvB, or p tgvAB expressing these genes from their native promoter is shown. B. Growth curves for the pEVS empty vector, p tgvA , p tgvB , and p tgvAB , infected at time point 0 with T2 phage at an MOI of 0.01 are shown. The mean and standard error of four biological replicates each with three technical replicates are presented. C. T2 was spotted as a tenfold serial dilution plaque assay on the empty vector, p tgvA (pBAD) , p tgvB (pBAD) , and p tgvAB (pBAD) expressed using an arabinose-inducible P BAD promoter. D. The graph shows PFU/mL for T2, T4, T5, T6, T7, secΦ17, secΦ27, and λ infected on E. coli containing the empty vector (pEVS) or p tgvAB . The dashed line denotes the limit of detection (LOD). A Welch’s t -test was used to compare the mean (three biological replicates each with three technical replicates) PFU count for each phage between the empty vector and p tgvAB . Due to the 8-basepair overlap between the tgvA and tgvB ORFs , we considered the possibility that the expression of tgvB would be disrupted if tgvA was not present due to regulatory mechanisms like translational coupling. To account for such a possibility, we created overexpression plasmids encoding tgvA, tgvB, and tgvAB expressed from an arabinose-inducible promoter and again screened for defense against T2 infection in E. coli . Plasmids overexpressing the individual genes, p tgvA (pBAD) and p tgvB (pBAD) , had plaquing equivalent to the empty vector, again supporting that tgvA and tgvB expression alone was insufficient for protection. However, p tgvAB (pBAD) showed full protection, demonstrating that both genes are necessary and sufficient to provide phage defense, whether they are expressed from their native promoters or from an arabinose-inducible promoter ( Fig. 3C ). Observing that p tgvAB was sufficient for protection against T2, we asked if tgvAB was responsible for robust protection against T2, T4, T5, T6, and secΦ18 and moderate protection against T3, secΦ18, secΦ27, and λ that we observed for pJBG007 ( Fig. 1C ). We thus tested the infection of p tgvAB and pEVS against all 10 coliphage and only observed robust protection against T2, T4, and T6 with p tgvAB ( Fig. 3D ). Based on these data, we conclude that tgvAB together is sufficient for protection against T-even coliphages, but other genes on pJBG007 are required for protection against T3, T5, secΦ18, secΦ27, and λ. We hypothesized that the hsdMSR Type I R-M system that tgvAB is embedded in could be responsible for protection to other phages ( Fig. 1D ). To test this, we amplified the hsdMSR genes using the Δ tgvAB cosmid as a template and cloned these onto a plasmid such that the Type I R-M system would be functional and tgvAB was inactive. We then exposed this plasmid, designated phsdMSR (Δ tgvAB ), to ten coliphage. Protection to T2, T4, and T6 was lost, as expected, but this plasmid provided significant protection against T3, secΦ18, secΦ27 and λ, demonstrating that indeed the hsdMSR Type I R-M system is functional and can protect against some phages ( Fig. 4 ). T5 is the only phage that is restricted by pJGB007 but is not affected by tgvAB or hsdMSR , and the mechanism in pJGB007 that targets this phage remains to be determined. Fig 4 Fig 4 Type-I restriction system protects against a variety of phages. Tenfold serial dilution plaque assay of ten phages spotted on E. coli encoding pEV (empty vector) or p hsdMSR (Δ tgvAB ). Plaque reduction is indicated by red boxes that are set at the lowest dilution that plaques in the p hsdMSR (Δ tgvAB ) strain to aid in comparison between the two strains.

T2 escapes TgvAB defense by mutations in α-glucosyltransferase ( agt ) Our results thus far suggest that TgvAB is a Type IV restriction system that likely digests modified phage DNA. However, it was unclear which specific base modifications were targeted. To gain further understanding of tgvAB defense, we passaged T2 on E. coli containing p tgvAB in five independently evolved populations to isolate resistant mutations. These five populations were pooled together and monitored for susceptibility to p tgvAB . From this procedure, we isolated four T2 plaques from this pool that formed plaques on E. coli carrying p tgvAB . In classical R-M systems, phage DNA passaged in the presence of the modification enzyme can be modified such that it avoids restriction in subsequent infections. These modifications, however, are lost if the phage is passaged in a host lacking the R-M system ( 47 ). To test whether these four individual T2 isolates escaped protection from tgvAB due to genome modifications mediated by tgvAB , we passaged all four individual T2 mutants on E. coli without tgvAB and isolated the phage. We then infected E. coli containing p tgvAB or the empty vector (pEVS) and observed that all four T2 mutants still formed plaques in the presence of p tgvAB, whereas WT T2 only plaqued in its absence ( Fig. 5A ). This result suggests that these phages had acquired resistant mutations. Similarly, all four T2 resistant mutants were insensitive to TgvAB in the liquid culture (Extended Data Fig. 5A ). Fig 5 Fig 5 T2 escapes TgvAB defense by mutations in α-glucosyltransferase ( agt ). A. A plaquing assay of tenfold serial dilution of WT T2 and evolved T2 mutants spotted on the empty vector or a p tgvAB host. B. Schematic of the agt gene in T2 indicates the position of the sequenced T2 mutations. C. The nucleotide sequence that is mutated and surrounding region in agt is compared to the sequence of agt from WT T2. Regions that are highlighted in red indicate a deletion of a T in that position. D. A plaquing assay of tenfold serial dilutions of WT T2 and T2 mutants passaged on the host expressing p agt T2 with a P BAD promoter on the empty vector or a p tgvAB host is shown. Lowest phage dilution is indicated by black boxes for pEVS and red boxes for p tgvAB . Due to our analysis that predicted tgvAB belongs to the GmrSD/DndB/SspE family, all of which specifically digest phage DNA with unique modifications to 5-hydroxymethylcytosine (5hmC) ( 27 , 29 , 48 ), we hypothesized that the T2 tgvAB- resistant mutants had nonfunctional mutations in genes that were associated with unique modifications to 5hmC. We focused on the T2 gene agt , which encodes an α-glucosyltransferase that glucosylates 5hmC bases, due to prior studies showing the homologous GmrSD targets glucosylated phage DNA with these modifications and agt modifies 70% of the cytosines of T4 during an infection ( 29 , 37 ). We sequenced the T2 agt gene for all four T2 mutants and a T2 control that was passaged in E. coli that did not have tgvAB and discovered each T2-resistant phage had a mutation in agt, whereas the T2 control did not ( Fig. 5B ). Upon further analysis, all four mutants had a single-nucleotide deletion of a thymine at three distinct poly-T tracts in the agt gene ( Fig. 5C ). We then confirmed that all these mutants had deletion of a thymine using whole-genome sequencing. The T2 Mut2 showed two additional mutations not found in T2 Mut6, indicating these were independent mutations, even though they delete a thymine in the same poly-T track ( Table S4 ). Therefore, our sequencing analysis indicated that we isolated four independent tgvAB T2-resistant mutants that occurred in agt, and glucosylation of 5hmC of phage DNA is important for tgvAB defense. To further explore the role of agt , we asked whether overexpression of T2 agt from a plasmid (p agt T2 ) in E. coli could restore glucosylation of 5hmC of the T2-resistant mutants, rendering them susceptible to tgvAB . All four T2 mutants and WT T2 were passaged in the E. coli strain containing p agt T2 . As expected, WT T2 remained sensitive to p tgvAB as it encodes a functional agt . Passage of the T2-resistant phage in E. coli with p agt T2 increased the susceptibility of these mutants to tgvAB by ~10–100-fold ( Fig. 5D ). Taken together, these data suggest that TgvAB targets α-glucosylated 5hmC bases in T2 phage, and loss of this modification renders T2 completely resistant to TgvAB.

T4 Δ agt Δ bgt escapes TgvAB defense Based on our previous results, we observed that tgvAB also confers protection against T4 and T6, each which have glucosylated 5hmC bases. We therefore explored the role of glucosylated 5hmC in tgvAB defense against T4. Indeed, liquid infection of T4 at an MOI of 0.01 in E. coli strains containing p tgvA, p tgvB, p tgvAB , and or the pEVS empty vector indicated that both tgvA and tgvB were required for phage defense, analogous to T2 ( Fig. 6A ). These results were confirmed using an EOP assay ( Fig. 6B ). We then asked whether T4 was able to escape tgvAB protection if it can no longer glucosylate 5hmC, similar to the T2 TgvAB-resistant mutants. To test this, we used a mutant T4 phage deleted for both agt and a second gene, bgt, that has β-glucosyltransferase activity on 5hmC (gift of Mike Laub). Prior studies have shown that both agt and bgt are required for glucosylation of 5hmC in T4 with agt α-glucosylating 70% of 5hmCs, while bgt β-glucosylates 30% of these bases ( 37 ). Infection of E. coli containing p tgvAB or the empty vector (pEVS) with T4 Δ agt Δ bgt showed that this phage was unaffected by tgvAB ( Fig. 6C ). Restoration of glucosylation of 5hmC by passage of the T4 Δ agt Δ bgt mutant in E. coli strains overexpressing either T4 agt (p agt T4 ) or bgt (p bgt T4 ) restored partial susceptibility to tgvAB ( Fig. 6C ). Together, these data suggest that either α- or β- glucosylation on 5hmC of T4 DNA is required for tgvAB recognition of T4, similar to our results for T2. Fig 6 Fig 6 TgvAB protects against T4 infection and T4 Δ agt Δ bgt escapes TgvAB defense. A. E. coli encoding p tgvA , p tgvB , and p tgvAB was infected with T4 at an MOI of 0.01 at time 0. The mean and standard error of four biological replicates each with three technical replicates are presented. B. Tenfold serial dilutions of T4 were spotted on E. coli containing the empty vector (pEVS), p tgvA , p tgvB , and p tgvAB . C. Tenfold serial dilution of T4 Δ agt Δ bgt (top), T4 Δ agt Δ bgt were passaged on the host expressing p agt T4 (middle), or p bgt T4 (bottom) with a P BAD promoter and spotted on E. coli containing the empty vector (pEVS) or p tgvAB .

Although the loss of agt and/or bgt may provide resistance to TgvAB defense, we hypothesized the unglucosylated 5hmC may now become sensitive to other Type IV restriction systems that digest unglucosylated 5hmC, such as the mcrA and mcrBC restriction systems encoded in E. coli strain M1655. We thus infected MG1655 with T2 and all four of the tgvAB resistance mutants. We observed that WT T2 showed substantial plaquing in MG1655; however, all the T2-resistant mutants exhibited little to no plaques ( Fig. 7A ). These results suggest that MG1655 restricts the T2 agt mutants but is unable to restrict WT T2. This result also further demonstrates loss of glucosylation of 5hmC in our T2 resistance mutants. Fig 7 Fig 7 T2 and T4 mutants are susceptible to McrA and McrBC, respectively. A. Tenfold serial dilution of WT T2 and the four evolved T2 mutants were spotted on MG1655 WT or a MG1655 Δ mcrA Δ mcrBC host. B. The evolved T2 mutants were passaged on a host expressing p agt T2 and then spotted as tenfold dilutions onto MG1655 WT or a MG1655 Δ mcrA Δ mcrBC host. C. WT T4, T4 Δ agt Δ bgt , and T4 Δ agt Δ bgt were passaged on the host expressing p agt T4 , or p bgt T4 with a P BAD promoter and then spotted as tenfold dilutions on MG1655 WT and MG1655 Δ mcrA Δ mcrBC host. We hypothesized that removal of mcrA and mcrBC would rescue the T2 resistant mutants’ infectivity. Indeed, infection of MG1655 Δ mcrA Δ mcrBC (gift of Mike Laub) with all T2 mutants and WT T2 showed robust plaquing ( Fig. 7A ). To test whether we could restore the sensitivity of the T2-resistant mutants to mcrA and mcrBC , we infected MG1655 with T2 mutants passaged in the presence of p agt T2 and observed 100–1,000-fold increased plaquing ( Fig. 7B ). These data further support that the T2 resistance agt mutants we isolated no longer glucosylate 5hmC and thus become sensitive to another Type IV restriction enzyme that targets unglucosylated 5hmC. To determine if the above observations are specific to T2, we explored the sensitivity of T4 Δ agt Δ bgt to mcrA and mcrBC . Infection of MG1655 with T4 and T4 Δ agt Δ bgt showed little to no plaques of T4 Δ agt Δ bgt, whereas WT T4 exhibited substantial plaquing ( Fig. 7C ). However, infection of MG1655 with T4 Δ agt Δ bgt passaged on either p agt T4 or p bgt T4 showed 100–1,000-fold increased plaquing ( Fig. 7C ). Thus, like T2, resistance to TgvAB via the loss of 5hmC glucosylation in T4 has an evolutionary tradeoff of increased sensitivity to other Type IV restriction systems like McrA/McrBC.

DISCUSSION.content: V. choleraetransitions between life in environmental reservoirs and infection of human hosts to cause cholera, and in each of these environments, it has intricate interactions with phage (3). Although several phage defense systems have been discovered in this bacterium, there is still more to learn about howV. choleraedefends itself against phage infection.

VPI-2 is a pathogenicity island that is only present in toxigenicV. choleraeO1 and O139 serogroup isolates (9,10). The acquisition of VPI-2 has been studied extensively; however, the biological function of most of the genes on VPI-2 remains unclear. In this work, we identified a region of VPI-2 that protectsE. colifrom infection by T2, T4, T5, T6 and secΦ18 and significant protection to several other coli phage via two distinct R-M systems (Fig. 1C). Along withddmDEand a putative Zorya module, this cluster of defense systems within VPI-2 support prior studies that have found that phage defense systems frequently cluster together in phage defense islands (41–44). Furthermore, the identification of this phage defense cluster supports the notion that acquisition of VPI-2 contributed to long-term environmental persistence ofV. cholerae(49).

TgvAB protects from phage infection by targeting glucosylation of 5hmC, and loss of this gluosylation renders T2 and T4 completely resistant to TgvAB (Fig. 8). BLAST analysis of these genes showed that TgvA and TgvB belong to a conserved family of Type IV DNase/DNA nickases specific for phosphorothioated or glycosylated phage DNA. Within this conserved family is GmrSD, a Type IV modification-dependent system which targets glucosylated 5hmC bases (29,46). GmrS encodes a DUF262 domain, while GmrD encodes a DUF1524 domain. DUF1524 contains a conserved HXXP motif that is seen in protein families such as the His-Me finger endonuclease superfamily (50). DUF262 is a member of the ParB-like superfamily that includes nucleases related to ParB in addition to uncharacterized proteins (46). Notably, the DUF262 region within GmrSD has been shown to encode a highly conserved DGQQR motif that is responsible for NTP binding and hydrolysis, which could be important for DNA cleavage (46). Studies have shown that a fused form of GmrSD, in which the two domains are linked as one polypeptide, is more abundant than systems where GmrS and GmrD are encoded as separate polypeptides (46). However, in either case, both GmrSD together are required for phage defense. TgvAB is distinct from this system, in that TgvA only contains a DUF262 domain, while TgvB contains both a DUF262 and DUF1524 region. Additionally, both TgvA and TgvB encode the DGQQR motif, suggesting TgvB is a GmrSD-fused polypeptide that alone should be sufficient for phage defense; however, the expression of TgvA or TgvB alone with their native promoters or overexpressed using the pBAD promoter was insufficient for defense. Phage defense only occurred when both TgvA and TgvB were present. It is possible that TgvA and TgvB form a complex that is required for recognition and restriction of modified phage DNA. In support of this, the ORFs oftgvAandtgvBoverlap by 8 basepairs, further suggesting a connection either through regulation of expression or some other mechanism. However, more studies need to be done to elucidate the molecular mechanism of TgvAB and how it specifically targets glucosylated 5hmC.

Fig 8Fig 8Model for TgvAB defense against phage and evolutionary tradeoffs ofagt-resistant mutants. In a cell expressing Type IV restriction systems like TgvAB, McrA, and McrBC, when a phage adsorbs and injects its DNA, if the phage DNA is glucosylated at 5hmC bases, TgvAB recognizes this modification and restricts phage DNA to inhibit phage progeny (middle). If the phage DNA is injected and is not glucosylated, only having a 5hmC modification, it is resistant to TgvAB. However, McrA and McrBC recognize this mutant phage and restrict phage DNA, inhibiting phage progeny (right).

Studies have shown that GmrSD targets and restricts glucosylated 5hmC phage DNA (29), and we report a similar result for TgvAB using both T2 and T4. α- and β-glucosylation of 5hmC occurs in all T-even phages and is encoded byagtandbgt,respectively, in both T2 and T4. By evolving T2 in the presence of TgvAB,we identified four independent null mutations in the T2agtgene that provide resistance to TgvAB protection, suggesting that α-glucosylation is critical for T2 sensitivity to TgvAB. Additionally, we show that a T4ΔagtΔbgtmutant was able to bypass TgvAB defense (Fig. 5C). Partial susceptibility to TgvAB can be restored by passaging T4ΔagtΔbgtin a strain overexpressing either T4agtor T4bgt, demonstrating that TgvAB can target both α- and β-glucosylated 5hmC. Further studies are needed to deduce how TgvAB targets α- and/or β-glucose modification to 5hmC in different phages.

McrA and McrBC both separately target 5hmC and 5mC modifications, respectively (51,52). We demonstrated that there is a tradeoff for mutatingagt,as losing the glucose modification to 5hmC renders T2 and T4 susceptible to recognition and restriction by other Type IV restriction systems such as McrA and McrBC. This suggests that phage can transition between glucosylated and unglucosylated DNA to evade different Type IV modification systems (Fig. 8). Interestingly, all T2 mutations occurred in poly-T tracts, suggesting these sites might be contingency loci allowing for rapid evolution of genome modifications in response to different evolutionary pressures imposed by restriction enzymes, an idea which we are further exploring. Although all these systems have been extensively studied, R-M systems have only recently begun to be identified inV. cholerae(23,53,54).

This study identifies a novel Type IV restriction system inV. cholerae. We note that an independent study concurrently identified TgvAB and reached analogous conclusions (55).V. choleraeis predated by three major lytic phages, ICP1, ICP2, and ICP3 (3). Using BLAST, we searched foragtorbgthomologs in 86V.choleraephages identified by Ranjan et al. (56). However, none of these phages encode obviousagtorbgtcandidates, and thus we predict they would be resistant to TgvAB. Furthermore, we overexpressed ptgvABand pEV inV. choleraestrain E7946 and infected with ICP1, ICP2, and ICP3 and found no differences in plaquing (Extended DataFig. 8). However, it remains possible that TgvAB protectsV. choleraefrom infection by other undiscovered phages, and it would be interesting to search for phages that can infect a ΔtgvAB V. choleraemutant but are restricted in the WT strain. Thus, additional studies to elucidate the function of TgvAB in its native context inV. choleraeand its impacts on phage infection are warranted.

MATERIALS AND METHODS.content: All bacterial and phage strains are listed in Table S1 . Unless otherwise stated, cultures were grown in lysogeny broth (LB) at 37°C in a shaking incubator set at 210 rpm and supplemented with the following antibiotics for plasmid maintenance: tetracycline (10 µg mL −1 ), kanamycin (100 µg mL −1 ), chloramphenicol (100 µg mL −1 ) and carbenicillin (100 µg mL −1 ) as necessary. Ectopic expression was induced with arabinose (0.5 mM) or IPTG (100 µM). Coliphages were propagated by streaking on MMB agar (LB +0.1 mM MnCl 2 +5 mM CaCl 2 +5 mM MgCl 2 +0.5% agar) with E. coli DH10β at a 1:1,000 dilution of an overnight culture and incubated overnight. These plates were then covered with 10 mL of phage buffer (0.1 mM Tris HCl 7.5 pH +10 mM MgSO 4 +0.4% NaCl 2 + dH 2 O) and incubated statically at 4°C for 48 hours. Phage buffer was then filtered through a 0.22-µM filter. Overnight cultures were prepared in the same media used in the experiment.

The V. cholerae pLAFR cosmid library was previously described ( 18 ). Overnight cultures of the cosmid library in E. coli strain DH5⍺ were serially plated at dilutions of 10 −4 , 10 −5 , and 10 −6 on LB +tetracycline agar plates. Isolated colonies were grown in a 96-well plate with LB +tetracycline at 37°C in a shaking incubator overnight. Overnight cultures of the library and pLAFR( 57 , 58 ) (a broad host range, tetracycline-resistant cosmid cloning vector) were then mixed 1:1,000 with 5 mL of MMB agar +tetracycline and poured into 4-well Nunc rectangular dishes (ThermoFisher). Dishes were dried at room temperature for 1 hour, and then 5 µL of tenfold serial dilutions of T2 stored in LB was spotted, air-dried, and incubated overnight at 37°C. Plaques from individual colonies were compared to plaques on pLAFR. All cosmids conferring T2 protection were extracted as described in plasmid construction, and gene identification was confirmed using Oxford Nanopore Technology through the sequencing company, Plasmidsaurus.

Overnight cultures with appropriate plasmids were mixed 1:1,000 with 20 mL of MMB agar +corresponding antibiotic and poured into 150 × 100 mm Petri dishes. Petri dishes were dried at room temperature for 1 hour. Five microliters of a tenfold dilution of corresponding coliphage in LB was spotted on top of the agar. Plaques were visualized using a Proteinsimple AlphaImager HP. All experiments were performed independently at least three times.

The coliphage titer was determined using plaque assay as previously described ( 59 ). Overnight cultures with appropriate plasmids were mixed with the lowest concentration of coliphage (180 µL of culture with 20 µL of phage) where plaques were still visualized. Culture and phage were then mixed 1:1,000 with 20 mL of MMB agar +corresponding antibiotic into 100 × 15 mm Petri dishes. Once plates solidified, they were incubated at 37°C overnight. Plaques were counted the next day. Data presented were calculated using Welch’s t -test and are representative of six biological replicates for pJBG007 and three biological replicates for p tgvAB . Three technical replicates were done for each biological replicate.

Overnight cultures with appropriate plasmids were diluted to an OD 600 of 0.01 into LB supplemented with antibiotics. Cultures were grown for ~3 hours to an OD 600 of 0.1. Once cultures reach an OD 600 of 0.1, they were aliquoted into a 96-well plate and infected at an MOI of 0.01 in LB at 37°C. Growth was monitored by measuring OD 600 every 2.5 minutes for 8 hours using a BioTek 800 TS (Agilent) with continuous, linear shaking. Data presented are the mean and standard error of four biological replicates each with three technical replicates.

Overnight cultures of p tgvAB and pMJF103 were diluted to an OD 600 of 0.01 into LB +kanamycin. Cultures were grown for ~3 hours to an OD 600 of 0.1 and then split into 10 separate flasks, five containing p tgvAB and five containing pMJF103. Cultures were infected with tenfold serial dilutions in LB of T2 with the highest MOI at 10. Flasks were incubated for 4 hours at 37°C in a shaking incubator at 210 rpm. After 4 hours, the cultures were pooled and filtered using a 0.22-µM filter. Overnight culture of p tgvAB was mixed 1:1,000 with 20 mL MMB agar +kanamycin and dried at room temperature for 1 hour. T2 lysates passaged on p tgvAB and pMJF103 were streaked for isolated plaques and placed at 37°C overnight. Five isolated plaques from streak plates were picked, resuspended in 1 mL of phage buffer, and left static at 4°C for 24 hours. Overnight culture of p tgvAB was mixed 1:1,000 with 20 mL MMB agar +kanamycin and dried at 23°C for 1 hour. Isolated plaques suspended in phage buffer were streaked and placed at 37°C overnight. Ten milliliters of phage buffer was added to each plate and left static at 4°C for 48 hours. Five microliters of a tenfold dilution of each T2 lysates was spotted on top of the MMB agar mixed 1:1,000 with p tgvAB to test for resistance. All lysates mentioned were passaged as mentioned above one more time on pMJF103. All lysates were used for further analysis and sequencing as described below. The culture passaged on only pMJF103 was also analyzed. To extract phage DNA, 500-μL phage lysates suspended in phage buffer were incubated at 98°C for 10 minutes to burst open the phage capsid head and inactivate any residual DNase and RNase from the bacterial culture. Samples were further purified using the Wizard Genomic DNA Purification Kit (Promega) following the protocol for isolation of genomic DNA from Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria starting at step 3, under Lyse Cells. The resulting DNA was PCR-amplified for agt using oJBG0129 and oJBG0120 and bgt genes using oJBG0127 and oJBG0128. PCR products were cleaned and purified using the DNA Clean & Concentrator kit (Zymo Research) and Oxford Nanopore-sequenced. Sequencing results were mapped to the T2 reference genome ( NC_054931.1 ) using Geneious Prime 2023.0.2.

All primers are listed in Table S3 , and plasmids are listed in Table S2 . All PCR products were amplified using Q5-High Fidelity DNA polymerase according to the manufacturer’s instructions (New England Biolabs). pMJF103 was constructed as an arabinose-inducible vector that could be conjugated. The pEVS141 vector backbone encoding the kanamycin resistance marker, p15A oriV and oriT, was amplified with primers oMJF043 and oMJF044. An arabinose-inducible cassette including araC and mOrange under the pBAD promoter was amplified from the construct pBAD_mOrange (a gift of Patricia Champion) using Q5 and primers oMJF041 and oMJF042. Amplifications were confirmed by gel electrophoresis and treated with DpnI enzyme to remove the template. Products were mixed 1:1 and transformed into chemically competent DH5α E. coli and plated on LB agar with 100 µg/mL kanamycin. Transformants were picked for overnight broth culture in LB supplemented with 100 µg/mL kanamycin and 0.01% L-arabinose. Cultures showing mOrange expression were subsequently miniprepped using a Wizard miniprep kit (Promega). To remove the mOrange insert and generate and empty cloning vector, the plasmid was double-digested with NdeI and SpeI restriction enzymes (NEB) followed by gel purification of the 4725-bp band using a Wizard spin column (Promega). A multiple cloning site linker was prepared by annealing the primers oMJF055 and oMJF056 at 8 µM each in a buffer of 50 mM Tris HCl pH7.5, 50 mM NaCl, and 1 mM EDTA. The linker was ligated into the purified linearized vector using T4 ligase (NEB) at 4°C overnight followed by transformation into DH5α E. coli . Presence and orientation of the linker was confirmed by colony PCR using GoTaq polymerase (Promega) with primers oMJF042 and oMJF055. Colonies with confirmed inserts were grown overnight in LB broth with 100 µg/mL kanamycin, and plasmids were purified by miniprep. To generate the p tgvAB , tgvAB was amplified from pJBG007 with the 520-bp upstream sequence to include its native promoter and overlapping ends to the pMJF103 using oJBG122 and oJBG106. To generate p tgvA, tgvA was amplified from pJBG007 with the 520-bp upstream sequence to include its native promoter and overlapping ends to pMJF103 using oJBG0122 and oJBG108. To generate ptgvB, tgvB was amplified from pJBG029 with the 520-bp upstream sequence to include its native promoter and overlapping ends to pMJF103 using oJBG122 and oJBG106. To generate the p hsdMSR , hsdMSR was amplified from pJBG023 with the 500-bp upstream sequence to include its native promoter and overlapping ends to the pMJF103 using oJBG154 and oJBG155. To generate T2 p agt, agt was amplified from T2 wild-type with overlapping ends to pMJF103 using oJBG145 and oJBG146. To generate T4 p agt, agt was amplified from T4 wild-type with overlapping ends to pMJF103 using oJBG141 and oJBG142. To generate T4 p bgt, bgt was amplified from T4 wild-type with overlapping ends to pMJF103 using oJBG139 and oJBG140. The pMJF103 backbone was linearized using primers oMJF019 and oMJF053. All PCR products were cleaned and purified using the DNA Clean & Concentrator kit (Zymo Research). All plasmid constructs were made using fast cloning ( 60 ). Amplified inserts and linearized pMJF103 backbone were DpnI-treated for at 37°C for 1 hour followed by heat inactivation at 80°C for 20 minutes. Following DpnI treatment, 1 µL of corresponding insert +1 µL of linearized pMJF103 backbone +50 µL of DH5α chemical competent cells were mixed and left on ice for 30 minutes. After 30 minutes, tubes were incubated at 42°C for 1 minute and immediately placed on ice for 3 minutes. About 500 µL of S.O.C was added, and cells were recovered at 37°C for 1 hour. Cells were spread on LB agar +kanamycin and left at 37°C overnight. Inserts were verified by PCR amplification of the cloning site using oMJF057 and oMJF058 primers. To generate E7946 with p tgvAB and E7946 with pEV, overnight cultures of BW29427 with p tgvAB or pEV were supplemented with 300 µg/mL of diaminopimelic acid (DAP) and mixed with an overnight culture of E7946 at a 1:1 ratio (25 µL each) and spotted on an LB Agar + DAP plate. The spot was dried and incubated at 37°C for 3 hours. The culture was struck on LB agar +kanamycin plates. A single colony was picked for p tgvAB and pEV. To extract plasmids, the Wizard Plus SV Minipreps DNA Purification System kit (Promega) was used following the Centrifugation Protocol under Quick Protocol. Miniprepped plasmids were introduced into DH10β cells by electroporation. All plasmids were confirmed by sequencing (Plasmidsaurus). Plasmid sequencing results were mapped to V. cholerae C6706 Chromosome I (CP064350) or T2 reference genome ( NC_054931.1 ) using Geneious Prime 2023.0.2.

A transposon mutant library in pJBG007 was made using the EZ-Tn5 <KAN-2> Insertion Kit (Lucigen). The following components were used for a 10-µL reaction [EZ-Tn5 10X Reaction Buffer (1 µL) +1 µg pJBG007 cosmid DNA (1 µL) +EZ-Tn5 <KAN-2> Transposon undiluted (1 µL) +EZ-Tn5 Transposase (1 µL) +Nuclease Free H 2 O (6 µL)]. The reaction was incubated at 37°C for 2 hours. After 2 hours, 1 µL of the stop solution was added, and the reaction mixture was incubated at 70°C for 10 minutes. Two microliters of the reaction mixture was dialyzed for 15 minutes and then electroporated in 20 µL of DH10β cells. Transformed cells were resuspended in 500 µl of S.O.C (2% tryptone +0.5% yeast extract +10 mM NaCl +2.5 mM KCl +10 mM MgCl 2 +10 mM MgSO 4 +20 mM glucose) and recovered at 37°C shaking 210 rpm for 1 hour. About 200 µL of the recovered culture and five individual 1:10 dilutions were plated on LB +kanamycin + tetracycline agar plates and incubated at 37°C overnight. All colonies were scraped and resuspended in 750 µL of LB. About 250 µL of 80% glycerol was added to the 750 µL resuspension and frozen at −80°C.

Lambda red recombineering was used as previously described ( 61 – 63 ). DH10β pJBG007 were made electrocompetent as follows: An overnight culture of pJBG007 grown in yeast extract and nutrient broth (YENB) (0.75% Bacto Yeast Extract +0.80% Nutrient Broth, pH 7.5 using NaOH) +Tetracycline was back-diluted 1:100 into YENB +tetracycline and grown at 30°C to an OD 600 of ~0.5. Cells were washed three times at 4°C with 10% glycerol. Cells were resuspended in 10% glycerol, aliquoted, and stored at −80°C. pKD46 ( 64 ) was dialyzed for 15 minutes and electroporated into pJBG007 electrocompetent cells. About 500 µL of S.O.C was added to cells and recovered at 30°C for 20 minutes. Cells were plated on LB agar +tetracycline + carbenicillin and incubated at 30°C overnight. pJBG007 +pKD46 cells were grown in LB + tetracycline + carbenicillin and made electrocompetent as described above. The chloramphenicol acetyltransferase ( cat ) cassette was PCR-amplified from pKD3(64) with overlapping ends to the either tgvA using oJBG037 and oJBG038, tgvB using oJBG022 and oJBG023, or tgvAB using oJBG047 and oJBG048. All PCR products were cleaned and purified using the DNA Clean & Concentrator kit (Zymo Research). The amplified chloramphenicol cassette was dialyzed as previously described and electroporated into pJBG007 +pKD46 electrocompetent cells. About 500 µL of S.O.C was added to cells and recovered at 37°C for 1 hour. Cells were plated on LB agar +tetracycline + chloramphenicol and incubated at 37°C overnight. The insert was verified by PCR amplification using primers using oJBG021 and oJBG030 that amplified the outside of the insertion site. Overnight cultures of BW29427 with pTL17 supplemented with 300 µg/mL of diaminopimelic acid (DAP) was mixed with an overnight of pJBG007 +CAM cassette at a 1:1 ratio (25 µL each) and spotted on an LB Agar + DAP plate. The spot was dried and incubated at 37°C for 3 hours. The culture was struck on LB agar +chloramphenicol + kanamycin plates. Overnight cultures of pJBG007 +cat cassette +pTL17 ( 65 ) were back-diluted 1:100 into LB + chloramphenicol + kanamycin +IPTG and incubated at 37°C for 2 hours. Tenfold dilution of the culture was made, and dilutions from 10 3 to 10 9 were plated on LB agar +tetracycline plates at 37°C overnight. Patch plating starting with LB agar +tetracycline, LB agar +chloramphenicol, LB agar +kanamycin, and ending with LB agar was used to confirm removal of cat cassette and pTL17 from the host. Deletions are left with an FRT scar after removal of cat cassette. Deletions were confirmed by sequencing (Plasmidsaurus).

Overnight cultures of T2 p agt and p bgt and T4 p agt and p bgt were diluted 1:1000 in LB +kanamycin + arabinose and incubated for ~3 hours at 37°C. Cultures were then mixed 1:1,000 in 20 mL MMB agar +kanamycin + arabinose and dried at room temperature for 1 hour. All T2 mutants (mutants 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6) were streaked on both T2 p agt and p bgt, and T4 Δ agt Δ bgt were streaked on both T4 p agt and p bgt . Plates were then incubated at 37°C. Plates were covered with 10 mL phage buffer and left static at 4°C for 48 hours. Phage buffer was filtered through a 0.22-µM filter. Five microliters of a tenfold dilution of each T2 mutants and T4 Δ agt Δ bgt that were passaged on their corresponding p agt or p bgt was spotted on top of the MMB agar mixed 1:1,000 with either p tgvAB , pMJF103, MG1655, or MG1655 Δ mcrBC Δ mcrA .
